q: A nurse is caring for a patient at 28 weeks gestation with a blood pressure of 146/94 on two readings 5 hours apart. Urinalysis shows no proteinuria. Which condition does this presentation most likely represent?
rationale: Gestational hypertension is defined as new-onset BP ≥140/90 after 20 weeks gestation without proteinuria or end-organ damage. The two readings 5 hours apart (≥4 hours required) confirm the diagnosis. Preeclampsia requires proteinuria or end-organ dysfunction. Chronic HTN would present before 20 weeks.

q: Which statement best describes the relationship between the hypertensive disorders of pregnancy?
options: They are entirely separate diseases with different pathophysiology
They represent a spectrum of disease that can progress from mild to severe
HELLP syndrome always develops from eclampsia
Gestational hypertension never progresses to preeclampsia
rationale: The hypertensive disorders of pregnancy exist on a spectrum: Gestational HTN → Preeclampsia (without severe features) → Preeclampsia (with severe features) → Eclampsia and/or HELLP. Approximately 50% of gestational HTN cases progress to preeclampsia. HELLP can appear at any stage and does not require eclampsia first.

q: A patient at 32 weeks gestation has BP 152/96, proteinuria of 350 mg/24h, normal platelets, and normal liver enzymes. The nurse recognizes this as:
rationale: Preeclampsia without severe features requires BP ≥140/90 after 20 weeks plus proteinuria ≥300 mg/24h. This patient meets both criteria. Without features like BP ≥160/110, platelets <100K, elevated LFTs, or neurological symptoms, it is classified as without severe features.

q: What is the ONLY definitive cure for preeclampsia, eclampsia, and HELLP syndrome?
rationale: The only cure for preeclampsia, eclampsia, and HELLP is delivery of the placenta. All other interventions—MgSO4, antihypertensives, bed rest—are temporizing measures to stabilize the mother and buy time for fetal lung maturity. Symptoms can persist or even worsen up to 48 hours after delivery.

q: A nurse understands that approximately what percentage of patients with gestational hypertension will progress to preeclampsia?
rationale: Approximately 50% of patients diagnosed with gestational hypertension will progress to preeclampsia. This is why close monitoring, frequent lab assessments, and patient education about warning signs are critical even when the initial presentation seems mild.

q: Which finding differentiates preeclampsia from gestational hypertension?
options: Onset during pregnancy rather than before
Presence of proteinuria or end-organ dysfunction
rationale: Both gestational HTN and preeclampsia require new-onset BP ≥140/90 after 20 weeks. The key differentiator is that preeclampsia includes proteinuria (≥300 mg/24h) and/or end-organ dysfunction (thrombocytopenia, renal insufficiency, elevated LFTs, pulmonary edema, or cerebral/visual symptoms).

q: The nurse is reviewing lab results for a 34-week gestation patient. Which combination of findings is diagnostic for HELLP syndrome?
options: Hemolysis (LDH ≥600), elevated liver enzymes (AST ≥70), platelets <100,000
rationale: HELLP syndrome requires ALL three: Hemolysis (LDH ≥600, schistocytes, ↓haptoglobin), Elevated Liver enzymes (AST ≥70 or ≥2× normal), and Low Platelets (<100,000). Notably, HELLP can occur without severe hypertension or significant proteinuria.

q: A patient presents at 36 weeks with new-onset seizures. She was previously diagnosed with preeclampsia. The nurse identifies this as:
rationale: Eclampsia is defined as new-onset tonic-clonic seizures in a woman with preeclampsia (or gestational HTN with proteinuria) that cannot be attributed to other causes. The presence of prior preeclampsia diagnosis plus new-onset seizures = eclampsia, which is an obstetric emergency.

q: Which statement about eclampsia timing is correct?
options: Postpartum eclampsia typically occurs within 48 hours but can occur up to 6 weeks after delivery
Eclampsia can only occur during the antepartum period
Eclampsia always presents with BP ≥160/110 before seizure onset
Eclampsia only occurs in women over age 35
rationale: Eclampsia can occur antepartum (~53%), intrapartum (~19%), or postpartum (~28%). Postpartum eclampsia typically occurs within 48 hours but can present up to 6 weeks after delivery. Importantly, ~20% of eclamptic women have BP <140/90 before their seizure.

q: A nurse is educating a patient at 22 weeks about gestational hypertension. Which statement requires further teaching?
options: My blood pressure should be monitored regularly throughout pregnancy
This condition usually resolves within 12 weeks after delivery
I should report any headaches or visual changes immediately
There is no risk of this progressing to a more serious condition
rationale: The statement that there is no risk of progression requires correction. Approximately 50% of gestational HTN cases progress to preeclampsia. Patients must understand the escalation potential and report warning signs (headache, visual changes, RUQ pain, decreased urine output) immediately.

q: Which assessment finding is the defining event that distinguishes eclampsia from severe preeclampsia?
rationale: Seizures are the defining event of eclampsia. While severe preeclampsia may present with extreme hypertension, significant proteinuria, and thrombocytopenia, the occurrence of new-onset tonic-clonic seizures not attributable to other causes is what makes the diagnosis eclampsia.

q: A patient at 33 weeks with HELLP syndrome has a BP of 134/86. The nurse understands that this:
options: Is possible because approximately 15% of HELLP patients are normotensive
Rules out HELLP since hypertension must be severe
Means the patient actually has gestational hypertension, not HELLP
rationale: HELLP syndrome may present with normal or only mildly elevated BP in approximately 15% of cases. The diagnosis depends on the lab triad (hemolysis, elevated liver enzymes, low platelets), NOT on blood pressure severity. This is a common reason HELLP gets misdiagnosed.

q: Preeclampsia can be diagnosed in the absence of proteinuria when which of the following is present?
options: New-onset HTN plus thrombocytopenia, renal insufficiency, or elevated LFTs
rationale: Preeclampsia can be diagnosed without proteinuria if there is new-onset hypertension after 20 weeks plus any end-organ dysfunction: thrombocytopenia, renal insufficiency, elevated liver transaminases, pulmonary edema, or cerebral/visual symptoms. Edema alone is not diagnostic.

q: Which condition on the hypertensive spectrum carries the highest perinatal mortality rate?
rationale: HELLP syndrome carries the highest perinatal mortality at 7-60%, compared to eclampsia's 1-2% maternal mortality. HELLP's multi-organ crisis involving hepatic rupture, DIC, and placental abruption creates extreme danger for both mother and fetus.

q: When does preeclampsia characteristically develop?
options: After 20 weeks gestation, or up to 6 weeks postpartum
rationale: Preeclampsia characteristically develops after 20 weeks gestation. It can also present postpartum—up to 6 weeks after delivery. New-onset hypertension before 20 weeks typically suggests chronic hypertension rather than a gestational hypertensive disorder.

q: The pathophysiology of preeclampsia begins with:
options: Abnormal placentation with inadequate trophoblast invasion of spiral arteries
Fetal genetic abnormalities affecting hormone production
Maternal kidney disease causing fluid retention
rationale: Preeclampsia begins with abnormal placentation—inadequate trophoblast invasion of spiral arteries leads to placental ischemia, which triggers release of anti-angiogenic factors causing widespread endothelial dysfunction, vasospasm, increased permeability, and coagulation cascade activation. The placenta is the cause; the entire maternal vascular system is the target.

q: Which finding classifies preeclampsia as having severe features? Select the best answer.
rationale: Platelets <100,000 is one of the severe features. Other severe features include: BP ≥160/110, creatinine >1.1, AST/ALT ≥2× normal, pulmonary edema, persistent headache unrelieved by medication, and visual disturbances. Proteinuria amount does NOT determine severity. Edema alone is not a severe feature.

q: A nurse is assessing a patient with preeclampsia. Which finding would be MOST concerning for disease progression?
options: Weight gain of 1 pound this week
Persistent frontal headache unrelieved by acetaminophen
rationale: A persistent severe headache not relieved by medication is a warning sign of cerebral edema/vasospasm and indicates progression toward eclampsia. This is a severe feature of preeclampsia. Mild edema, modest weight gain, and BP 142/92 (not ≥160/110) are less concerning.

q: When should low-dose aspirin (81 mg daily) be started for preeclampsia prevention in high-risk women?
options: At the first positive pregnancy test
At 20 weeks gestation when screening begins
After the first elevated blood pressure reading
rationale: Low-dose aspirin 81 mg daily should be started at 12-16 weeks gestation for women with high-risk factors. This is a primary prevention measure. Once preeclampsia develops, aspirin is NOT the treatment. High-risk factors include prior preeclampsia, multifetal gestation, chronic HTN, diabetes, and renal disease.

q: A nulliparous patient asks which risk factors qualify her for aspirin prophylaxis. Which factor alone is sufficient to warrant aspirin?
options: Prior preeclampsia in a previous pregnancy
rationale: Prior preeclampsia is a high-risk factor—any single high-risk factor warrants aspirin prophylaxis. Nulliparity, age ≥35, and obesity are moderate-risk factors that require 2 or more to be present before aspirin is recommended.

q: Which symptom reported by a preeclamptic patient would alert the nurse to possible hepatic involvement?
options: Lower back pain radiating to the legs
Right upper quadrant or epigastric pain
Left-sided chest pain with deep breathing
rationale: RUQ or epigastric pain indicates hepatic capsule distension from hepatic edema—a sign of liver involvement and possible progression to HELLP. This is a severe feature of preeclampsia. It is commonly misdiagnosed as gastritis or gallbladder disease.

q: A patient with preeclampsia has a urine output of 22 mL/hr. The nurse recognizes this indicates:
options: Need to increase oral fluid intake only
rationale: Urine output <30 mL/hr indicates renal vasospasm with decreased perfusion. This is a concerning finding in preeclampsia that suggests worsening end-organ damage. The nurse should notify the provider, not simply increase fluids, as fluid overload can worsen pulmonary edema.

q: When assessing DTRs in a preeclamptic patient, the nurse finds 4+ (hyperactive) reflexes with clonus. This finding indicates:
options: CNS irritability and increased seizure risk
rationale: Hyperreflexia with clonus indicates CNS irritability and is a warning sign of impending seizure (eclampsia). Brisk DTRs, especially with clonus, require immediate notification of the provider and preparation for seizure prophylaxis with MgSO4. Loss of DTRs (not hyperreflexia) indicates magnesium toxicity.

q: A patient at 30 weeks with severe preeclampsia is stable on MgSO4. The expected delivery timing is:
options: Immediate cesarean delivery regardless of stability
At 37 weeks as with preeclampsia without severe features
After 39 weeks for optimal fetal lung maturity
At 34 weeks or sooner if the patient becomes unstable
rationale: For preeclampsia with severe features, the recommended delivery timing is at 34 weeks, or sooner if the patient becomes unstable. Without severe features, delivery is recommended at 37 weeks. Waiting until 39 weeks would expose both mother and fetus to unacceptable risk.

q: Which lab finding in a preeclamptic patient indicates microangiopathic hemolytic anemia?
rationale: Schistocytes (fragmented RBCs) on peripheral smear indicate microangiopathic hemolytic anemia—RBCs are being sheared by fibrin strands in damaged blood vessels. This finding suggests progression toward HELLP. Haptoglobin would be decreased (consumed by hemolysis), not elevated.

q: A preeclamptic patient's creatinine level is 1.3 mg/dL. The nurse interprets this as:
options: A severe feature of preeclampsia indicating renal impairment
Indicative of chronic kidney disease unrelated to preeclampsia
rationale: Serum creatinine >1.1 mg/dL (or doubling of baseline) is a severe feature of preeclampsia indicating renal impairment from vasospasm. Normal creatinine in pregnancy is actually lower than non-pregnant values, making 1.3 even more significant.

q: The nurse is monitoring a preeclamptic patient's uric acid level, which is 7.2 mg/dL. This finding:
options: Is normal during the third trimester
Indicates the patient has gout, not preeclampsia
Correlates with disease severity due to decreased renal clearance
rationale: Elevated uric acid (>6 mg/dL) in preeclampsia correlates with disease severity. It results from decreased renal clearance due to vasospasm. While not a diagnostic criterion, it is a useful marker for monitoring disease progression.

q: A patient at 36 weeks has preeclampsia without severe features. What is the recommended delivery timing?
rationale: For preeclampsia without severe features, the recommended delivery timing is at 37 weeks. This balances the risks of continued pregnancy (disease progression) against the benefits of further fetal maturity. Severe features would warrant delivery at 34 weeks or sooner.

q: Which patient is at HIGH risk for preeclampsia and qualifies for aspirin prophylaxis based on a SINGLE factor?
options: A patient with a family history of preeclampsia
rationale: Multifetal gestation (twins+) is a high-risk factor—a single high-risk factor is sufficient to start aspirin. Age ≥35, nulliparity, obesity, and family history are all moderate-risk factors requiring ≥2 to be present. Other high-risk factors include prior preeclampsia, chronic HTN, diabetes, renal disease, and autoimmune disease.

q: A nurse assesses a preeclamptic patient and finds sudden facial edema, rapid weight gain of 3 pounds this week, and decreased urine output. The priority action is to:
options: Elevate the patient's legs and increase oral fluids
Notify the provider immediately about potential disease progression
Apply compression stockings and reassess in 4 hours
Document findings and continue routine monitoring
rationale: Sudden facial/hand edema, rapid weight gain (>2 lb/week), and decreased urine output are warning signs of worsening preeclampsia. The nurse must notify the provider immediately. These findings suggest increasing capillary permeability and renal vasospasm, potentially indicating progression to severe disease.

q: The severity of preeclampsia is determined by the amount of proteinuria. This statement is:
options: False—severity does NOT correlate with proteinuria amount
True—higher protein indicates more severe disease
False—proteinuria is not associated with preeclampsia at all
rationale: The severity of preeclampsia does NOT correlate with the amount of proteinuria. The previous criterion of >5g/24h as a severe feature has been removed. Severity is determined by blood pressure, organ dysfunction (platelets, liver, kidneys), and neurological symptoms—not by proteinuria quantity.

q: Which visual symptom in a preeclamptic patient warrants IMMEDIATE provider notification?
options: Mild blurring when reading small print
Scotomata (spots in visual field) with photophobia
rationale: Scotomata (spots), photophobia, blurred/double vision, or temporary blindness are severe features indicating cerebral vasospasm and potential impending eclampsia. These require immediate notification and likely initiation of MgSO4 for seizure prophylaxis.

q: A preeclamptic patient's haptoglobin level is critically low. The nurse interprets this as evidence of:
rationale: Low haptoglobin indicates hemolysis—haptoglobin is consumed when it binds free hemoglobin released from destroyed RBCs. This is a key marker for HELLP syndrome, along with elevated LDH, schistocytes, and elevated indirect bilirubin.

q: Which autoimmune conditions increase the risk of preeclampsia? Select the best answer.
options: Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) and antiphospholipid syndrome (APS)
rationale: SLE and antiphospholipid syndrome are autoimmune diseases that increase preeclampsia risk and are classified as high-risk factors. These conditions affect vascular endothelium and coagulation, compounding the endothelial dysfunction central to preeclampsia pathophysiology.

q: During an eclamptic seizure, what is the PRIORITY nursing action?
options: Position the patient on her LEFT side and protect the airway
Insert a padded tongue blade to prevent tongue biting
rationale: During an eclamptic seizure, the priority is airway protection. Position on the left side (left lateral) to protect the airway, prevent aspiration, and maximize placental perfusion. NEVER insert anything in the mouth. Do NOT rush to the OR during an active seizure—stabilize mother first.

q: Which medication is the FIRST-LINE treatment for eclamptic seizures?
rationale: Magnesium sulfate is the first-line treatment for both preventing and treating eclamptic seizures. It is superior to phenytoin (which is an outdated practice and always the WRONG NCLEX answer for eclampsia). Diazepam or lorazepam are only used if MgSO4 fails.

q: A patient has an eclamptic seizure and the fetal heart rate drops to 90 bpm. The nurse should:
options: Prepare for emergency cesarean section immediately
Administer terbutaline to increase fetal heart rate
Understand this is expected transient bradycardia that usually resolves in 3-5 minutes
rationale: Transient fetal bradycardia during an eclamptic seizure is expected and usually resolves within 3-5 minutes after the seizure stops. Rushing to the OR during an active seizure increases maternal risk. Stabilize the mother first, then reassess fetal status. Delivery is planned after maternal stabilization.

q: If MgSO4 fails to control eclamptic seizures, which medication should be administered next?
options: Additional MgSO4 2g IV bolus, then lorazepam 4mg IV if still seizing
Calcium gluconate to reverse the MgSO4
rationale: If seizures recur, give an additional MgSO4 2g IV bolus over 5 minutes. If MgSO4 still fails, lorazepam 4mg IV or diazepam 5-10mg IV is the next step—prepare for intubation. Phenytoin is NEVER the correct answer for eclampsia.

q: Approximately what percentage of eclamptic women have a BP below 140/90 BEFORE their seizure?
rationale: Approximately 20% of eclamptic women have BP below 140/90 before their seizure. This is a critical NCLEX concept—eclampsia can occur without severe hypertension, making it essential to monitor all preeclamptic patients for prodromal symptoms regardless of BP readings.

q: Which prodromal symptoms should the nurse assess for as potential precursors to eclampsia?
options: Severe headache, visual changes, RUQ pain, and hyperreflexia with clonus
Increased appetite, insomnia, and mild back pain
Frequent urination, mild nausea, and fatigue
rationale: Prodromal symptoms of impending eclampsia include severe headache, visual changes (scotomata, blurred vision), RUQ pain (hepatic involvement), and hyperreflexia with clonus (CNS irritability). Recognizing these symptoms allows proactive seizure prophylaxis with MgSO4.

q: During emergency management of eclampsia, supplemental oxygen should be administered at what rate?
options: 8-10 L/min via non-rebreather mask targeting SpO2 >94%
rationale: During eclampsia management, supplemental O2 at 8-10 L via non-rebreather mask is administered to maintain SpO2 >94%. This ensures adequate oxygenation for both mother and fetus during and after the seizure. Nasal cannula at 2L would be insufficient for this emergency.

q: A nurse is caring for a postpartum patient who delivered 18 hours ago after preeclampsia. The patient reports a severe headache and blurred vision. The nurse should:
options: Recognize these as potential signs of postpartum eclampsia and notify the provider immediately
Administer acetaminophen and reassess in 1 hour
Attribute symptoms to normal postpartum fatigue and pain
Encourage rest in a dark room and increase fluids
rationale: Severe headache and blurred vision in a postpartum patient with a history of preeclampsia are warning signs of postpartum eclampsia. Postpartum eclampsia typically occurs within 48 hours of delivery. The nurse must notify the provider immediately for evaluation and possible MgSO4 initiation.

q: Which statement about phenytoin (Dilantin) use in eclampsia is TRUE?
options: It is the preferred first-line agent for eclamptic seizures
It is an outdated practice and MgSO4 is always the correct NCLEX answer
It is equally effective as MgSO4 and can be used interchangeably
It should be used when MgSO4 is contraindicated in renal failure
rationale: Phenytoin (Dilantin) should NEVER be given for eclamptic seizures. MgSO4 is superior to phenytoin for both treating and preventing eclamptic seizures. Phenytoin is an outdated practice. On the NCLEX, MgSO4 is always the correct answer for eclampsia.

q: The loading dose of MgSO4 for eclampsia management is:
rationale: The MgSO4 loading dose is 4-6g IV over 15-20 minutes, followed by a maintenance infusion of 1-2g/hr. The loading dose must be given slowly to prevent cardiovascular collapse. The maintenance infusion continues for 24-48 hours postpartum.

q: After an eclamptic seizure, when should delivery be planned?
options: After maternal stabilization—do not rush to the OR during active seizures
Immediately during the seizure via emergency cesarean
Wait at least 72 hours to allow for full recovery
Only if the fetal heart rate does not recover within 1 hour
rationale: Delivery should be planned after maternal stabilization—do NOT rush to cesarean section during an active seizure, as this increases maternal risk. Stabilize with MgSO4, protect the airway, ensure oxygenation, and assess fetal status. Once the mother is stable, proceed with delivery.

q: The nurse is documenting an eclamptic seizure. Which information is MOST important to record?
options: The patient's last meal and fluid intake
Seizure duration, characteristics, maternal and fetal response
The number of visitors in the room
rationale: Documentation should include seizure duration, characteristics (tonic-clonic pattern, body parts involved), level of consciousness, vital signs during/after, fetal heart rate response, uterine activity, and all interventions performed with timing. This information guides ongoing management.

q: Which nursing intervention is contraindicated during an eclamptic seizure?
options: Positioning the patient on her left side
Restraining the patient's extremities to prevent injury
Providing supplemental oxygen after the seizure
rationale: Restraining a patient during a seizure is contraindicated and can cause injury. Instead, protect from harm by raising padded side rails, removing dangerous objects, and positioning on the left side. Do NOT insert anything in the mouth. Call for help and administer MgSO4 per protocol.

q: A 28-year-old at 38 weeks presents to triage with a tonic-clonic seizure. She has no history of preeclampsia or epilepsy. Her BP is 168/112. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: New-onset tonic-clonic seizures in a pregnant patient after 20 weeks with elevated BP is eclampsia until proven otherwise. Even without a prior preeclampsia diagnosis, the combination of pregnancy, hypertension, and seizures fits the eclampsia definition. Prior diagnosis is not required.

q: HELLP syndrome is frequently misdiagnosed as which condition?
rationale: HELLP is frequently misdiagnosed as gastritis, gallbladder disease, or hepatitis because its hallmark presentation includes RUQ/epigastric pain (90% of cases), malaise, and nausea/vomiting. Any pregnant woman in the 3rd trimester with these symptoms must have CBC and LFTs checked.

q: A patient at 35 weeks presents with nausea, vomiting, and epigastric pain. BP is 148/94. The nurse's PRIORITY action is to:
options: Administer an antacid and reassess after meals
Order CBC with platelets, LDH, and AST/ALT immediately
Start IV fluids for presumed dehydration from vomiting
rationale: "Stomach flu" symptoms (nausea, vomiting, epigastric pain) with elevated BP in the third trimester = think HELLP, NOT gastritis. The priority is to obtain CBC with platelets, LDH, AST/ALT, and peripheral smear immediately. The classic triad of RUQ pain + low platelets + elevated LFTs = HELLP until proven otherwise.

q: Which lab value is required to meet the hemolysis criterion for HELLP diagnosis?
rationale: For the Hemolysis component of HELLP, LDH must be ≥600. Other hemolysis markers include schistocytes on peripheral smear, decreased haptoglobin, and elevated indirect bilirubin. LDH ≥600 specifically reflects the degree of microangiopathic hemolytic anemia.

q: What percentage of HELLP syndrome patients develop DIC (disseminated intravascular coagulation)?
rationale: 21-40% of HELLP patients develop DIC, a life-threatening coagulation crisis. DIC screening labs include fibrinogen, PT/PTT, and D-dimer. This high rate of DIC makes HELLP one of the most dangerous complications in obstetrics.

q: When should platelet transfusion be given in HELLP syndrome?
options: Only if platelets <20,000, or <50,000 before cesarean section
Routinely for all HELLP patients regardless of platelet count
rationale: Platelet transfusion in HELLP is indicated when platelets are <20,000 (risk of spontaneous bleeding) or <50,000 before cesarean section (surgical bleeding risk). Routine transfusion for all HELLP patients is not indicated as the consumed platelets would be rapidly destroyed.

q: The greatest danger specific to HELLP syndrome that distinguishes it from severe preeclampsia is:
rationale: Hepatic rupture is the most feared complication specific to HELLP, resulting from severe hepatocellular necrosis and subcapsular hematoma formation. The swollen, damaged liver capsule can rupture, causing massive internal hemorrhage. Other HELLP complications include DIC, placental abruption, and renal failure.

q: What is the delivery recommendation for HELLP syndrome?
options: Emergent delivery regardless of gestational age
Deliver at 34 weeks with steroid coverage
Expectant management with close monitoring until 39 weeks
rationale: HELLP requires emergent delivery regardless of gestational age. While betamethasone is given if <34 weeks for fetal lung maturity, delivery should NOT be delayed for steroids. HELLP carries 1-3% maternal mortality and up to 60% perinatal mortality—the benefits of delivery far outweigh the risks of prematurity.

q: A HELLP patient's peripheral blood smear shows numerous fragmented red blood cells. These are called:
rationale: Schistocytes are fragmented RBCs that are the hallmark of microangiopathic hemolytic anemia. In HELLP, they form as RBCs are mechanically sheared when passing through damaged blood vessels with fibrin deposits. Their presence on smear is diagnostic evidence of hemolysis.

q: Which statement about proteinuria in HELLP syndrome is correct?
options: Significant proteinuria is always present in HELLP
Proteinuria >5g/24h is required for HELLP diagnosis
Proteinuria may be absent in up to 13% of HELLP cases
HELLP cannot be diagnosed without concurrent proteinuria
rationale: Proteinuria may be absent in up to 13% of HELLP cases. HELLP is diagnosed by the lab triad (hemolysis, elevated liver enzymes, low platelets), NOT by blood pressure or proteinuria criteria. This is why HELLP can be easily missed when clinicians expect typical preeclampsia findings.

q: The nurse is monitoring a HELLP patient. Which assessment schedule is most appropriate for platelet counts?
rationale: In HELLP syndrome, platelets should be monitored every 6 hours due to the rapid consumptive thrombocytopenia. LDH and LFTs should be checked every 12 hours. Frequent monitoring is essential because platelet counts can drop precipitously, increasing hemorrhage risk.

q: A nurse prepares a HELLP patient for emergent delivery. Which blood products should be on standby?
options: Packed RBCs, platelets, and fresh frozen plasma
Vitamin K and protamine sulfate only
rationale: HELLP patients need packed RBCs (for anemia from hemolysis), platelets (for thrombocytopenia), and fresh frozen plasma (for coagulation factor replacement if DIC develops) on standby. The 21-40% DIC rate makes blood product availability critical.

q: The therapeutic serum magnesium level for seizure prophylaxis is:
rationale: The therapeutic range for MgSO4 is 4-7 mEq/L. Below 4 is subtherapeutic (inadequate seizure protection). At 8-10, DTRs are lost. At 10-12, respiratory depression occurs. Above 15, cardiac arrest is possible. This cascade must be memorized for NCLEX.

q: A patient on MgSO4 infusion has absent DTRs. What is the FIRST nursing action?
options: Increase IV fluid rate to dilute the magnesium
Notify the provider and wait for orders
rationale: Absent DTRs is the first sign of magnesium toxicity (serum level 8-10 mEq/L). The nurse's FIRST action is to STOP the infusion—this is a nursing judgment call that does not require a provider order. Then notify the provider. Calcium gluconate is the antidote but is given for more severe toxicity (respiratory depression).

q: The antidote for MgSO4 toxicity that must be kept at the bedside is:
rationale: Calcium gluconate 1g IV is the antidote for MgSO4 toxicity and must be kept at the bedside at all times during infusion. Naloxone reverses opioids, flumazenil reverses benzodiazepines, and protamine reverses heparin.

q: The nurse uses the "BURP" mnemonic before each assessment of a patient on MgSO4. What does the "U" stand for?
rationale: BURP: BP (check for hypotension), Urine output (must be ≥30 mL/hr—Mg is renally excreted, so decreased output increases toxicity risk), Reflexes (check DTRs—absent = hold infusion), Pulmonary (RR must be ≥12/min).

q: A patient on MgSO4 has a respiratory rate of 10 breaths/min. The nurse should:
options: Continue the infusion and reassess in 15 minutes
Decrease the infusion rate by half
Stop the infusion, administer calcium gluconate, and prepare for possible intubation
Administer supplemental oxygen and continue the infusion
rationale: RR <12 indicates respiratory depression from magnesium toxicity (level 10-12 mEq/L). The nurse must STOP the infusion immediately, administer calcium gluconate 1g IV, and prepare for intubation. Continuing or merely reducing the infusion could lead to respiratory arrest.

q: How long should MgSO4 be continued after delivery in an eclamptic patient?
rationale: MgSO4 should continue for 24-48 hours postpartum because the eclampsia risk persists during this period. Postpartum eclampsia can occur up to 48 hours after delivery, and the protective effect of magnesium is needed throughout this critical window.

q: Which is the FIRST-LINE antihypertensive for acute severe hypertension in pregnancy?
rationale: Labetalol is the first-line antihypertensive for acute severe HTN in pregnancy. It is a combined alpha/beta-blocker given 20mg IV initially, then escalated (40mg, 80mg) q10min, max 300mg. Hydralazine is second-line. Lisinopril (ACE inhibitor) is ABSOLUTELY CONTRAINDICATED in pregnancy.

q: A nurse is preparing to administer labetalol to a severely preeclamptic patient. Which condition would contraindicate this medication?
rationale: Labetalol is contraindicated in asthma because its beta-blocking effects can cause bronchospasm. It is also contraindicated in heart block and heart failure. In asthmatic patients, nifedipine or nicardipine would be alternatives.

q: Why are ACE inhibitors and ARBs ABSOLUTELY contraindicated in pregnancy?
options: They cause fatal fetal renal agenesis, oligohydramnios, and skull defects
They are ineffective at lowering blood pressure during pregnancy
They interact with MgSO4 causing cardiac arrest
rationale: ACE inhibitors (lisinopril, enalapril) and ARBs (losartan, valsartan) cause fatal fetal renal agenesis, oligohydramnios, skull defects, and neonatal death. They are contraindicated in ALL trimesters. Selecting an ACE/ARB for pregnancy hypertension is an automatic wrong answer on NCLEX.

q: A preeclamptic patient is receiving hydralazine and develops a worsening headache. The nurse should consider that:
options: The medication is clearly not working and should be increased immediately
The headache confirms the patient now has eclampsia
This side effect means hydralazine is contraindicated for this patient
The headache is an expected side effect of hydralazine and may not indicate worsening preeclampsia
rationale: Headache is a common side effect of hydralazine (a direct vasodilator), and it can mimic worsening preeclampsia. The nurse must differentiate between drug side effects and disease progression through comprehensive assessment including BP trends, reflexes, visual symptoms, and lab values.

q: At what serum magnesium level does cardiac arrest occur?
rationale: The magnesium toxicity cascade: 4-7 = therapeutic, 8-10 = loss of DTRs, 10-12 = respiratory depression, 12-15 = respiratory arrest, >15 mEq/L = cardiac arrest. This escalation must be memorized to guide monitoring decisions.

q: Which route of administration for nifedipine should be AVOIDED in pregnancy hypertension?
rationale: Sublingual nifedipine should be avoided due to unpredictable absorption that can cause precipitous blood pressure drops. The oral route (10-20mg PO q30min, max 50mg) is the approved administration method for pregnancy hypertension.

q: A patient receiving MgSO4 has a urine output of 25 mL over the past hour. The nurse should:
options: Continue the infusion as this output is acceptable
Double the IV maintenance fluid rate
Hold the infusion because urine output is below 30 mL/hr—magnesium is renally excreted
rationale: Urine output must be ≥30 mL/hr for patients on MgSO4 because magnesium is renally excreted. Decreased UO means magnesium will accumulate, increasing toxicity risk. The nurse should HOLD the infusion and notify the provider. This is a nursing judgment decision.

q: The nurse checks DTRs before each MgSO4 assessment. Which reflex is most commonly assessed?
rationale: The patellar (knee-jerk) reflex is the most commonly assessed DTR during MgSO4 monitoring. It is easy to access, reliable, and provides clear evidence of CNS depression. Absent patellar reflex = first sign of magnesium toxicity → STOP infusion.

q: Which medication is given as a second-line antihypertensive when labetalol is contraindicated?
rationale: Hydralazine is the second-line antihypertensive for pregnancy (5-10mg IV q15-20min, max 30mg). It is a direct vasodilator. Enalapril (ACE inhibitor) and losartan (ARB) are absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy. Nifedipine or nicardipine are alternatives when both labetalol and hydralazine are unsuitable.

q: Betamethasone is administered to a HELLP patient at 31 weeks. The nurse understands this is given to:
options: Treat the hemolysis component of HELLP
rationale: Betamethasone is a corticosteroid given to accelerate fetal lung maturity when delivery is anticipated before 34 weeks. In HELLP, it is administered but delivery should NOT be delayed to complete the steroid course—emergent delivery takes priority.

q: A severely preeclamptic patient on MgSO4 asks why she has a Foley catheter. The nurse explains it is needed to:
options: Prevent the patient from getting out of bed
Strictly monitor urine output as an indicator of magnesium clearance and renal function
rationale: A Foley catheter ensures strict hourly urine output monitoring. Since MgSO4 is renally excreted, urine output directly reflects magnesium clearance. Output <30 mL/hr means magnesium is accumulating → increased toxicity risk. The Foley also reflects renal perfusion status in preeclampsia.

q: The mechanism of action of MgSO4 in seizure prevention is:
options: Increasing GABA activity in the brain
Blocking NMDA receptors and reducing cerebral vasospasm
Decreasing acetylcholine release at neuromuscular junctions
Inhibiting sodium channels in cortical neurons
rationale: MgSO4 works as a CNS depressant by blocking NMDA receptors and reducing cerebral vasospasm, which raises the seizure threshold. It also causes smooth muscle relaxation (vasodilation → ↓BP, and tocolysis → may slow labor).

q: Nicardipine 5mg/hr IV is ordered for a preeclamptic patient. The nurse understands this drug is:
options: An ACE inhibitor used for chronic hypertension
A benzodiazepine used for seizure control
An IV calcium channel blocker used when labetalol is contraindicated
rationale: Nicardipine is an IV calcium channel blocker with a predictable dose-response. It is used when labetalol is contraindicated (e.g., asthma, heart block). It is titrated up every 5-15 minutes to achieve target blood pressure.

q: A nurse is preparing a room for a preeclamptic patient. Which of the following is the HIGHEST priority?
options: Ensuring the TV remote is within reach
Setting up a meal tray with low-sodium options
Implementing seizure precautions with padded side rails, suction at bedside, and dim lighting
rationale: Seizure precautions are the highest priority for any preeclamptic patient. This includes padded side rails up, suction at bedside, calcium gluconate at bedside, dim lighting, reduced stimuli, and MgSO4 prepared. These prevent injury if seizures occur.

q: A preeclamptic patient should be positioned in which position?
rationale: Left lateral position is preferred for preeclamptic patients. It relieves pressure on the inferior vena cava, maximizes placental perfusion, promotes venous return, and optimizes renal blood flow (which helps with MgSO4 clearance). Supine position compresses the vena cava and worsens hemodynamics.

q: The nurse is caring for a HELLP patient postpartum. What is the priority assessment?
options: Assessing for signs of bleeding from all sites and monitoring labs every 6-12 hours
rationale: Post-delivery HELLP care focuses on hemorrhage surveillance—assess for bleeding from IV sites, surgical incisions, gums, and vaginal sites. Monitor platelets every 6 hours and LDH/LFTs every 12 hours. DIC risk persists, and liver recovery is monitored by trending labs. Symptoms can worsen up to 48 hours after delivery.

q: A patient with preeclampsia without severe features is being monitored. Which assessment schedule is appropriate for blood pressure?
options: Every 15 minutes during the acute phase
Only if the patient reports symptoms
rationale: During the acute phase of preeclampsia management, BP should be monitored every 15 minutes. Additionally, DTRs should be assessed every 1-4 hours, UO monitored hourly, and daily labs (CBC, LFTs, creatinine) obtained. Frequency may decrease as the patient stabilizes.

q: A nurse is teaching a preeclamptic patient about warning signs to report. Which patient statement indicates understanding?
options: "I should report increased appetite and energy."
"I need to tell the nurse about headaches that won't go away, spots in my vision, or pain under my right ribs."
"Minor swelling in my face and hands is normal and nothing to worry about."
rationale: The correct response includes the key warning signs: persistent headache (cerebral edema), visual disturbances (scotomata), and RUQ pain (hepatic involvement). Facial/hand edema should be reported (not dismissed), and decreased urination indicates worsening renal vasospasm.

q: For a patient on MgSO4, the nurse should limit which of the following to reduce seizure risk?
rationale: To reduce seizure risk, the nurse should limit visitors, minimize noise, and reduce environmental stimuli. Keep the room dim, quiet, and calm. Excessive stimulation can lower the seizure threshold. This is a fundamental nursing intervention for seizure precautions in preeclampsia/eclampsia.

q: Which fetal complication is the #1 consequence of preeclampsia?
rationale: Preterm delivery is the #1 complication of preeclampsia for the fetus. Other fetal complications include IUGR (from chronic placental insufficiency), oligohydramnios (decreased renal perfusion), and placental abruption. Delivery is often necessary before full term to protect the mother.

q: A nurse is monitoring fetal status in a patient with severe preeclampsia. Which fetal surveillance methods should be used?
options: Daily kick counts, non-stress testing (NST), and continuous monitoring during acute episodes
rationale: Fetal surveillance in preeclampsia includes daily kick counts, non-stress testing (NST), and continuous fetal monitoring during acute episodes. Chronic placental insufficiency can cause IUGR and oligohydramnios, making ongoing fetal assessment essential.

q: A patient at 33 weeks with severe preeclampsia is on MgSO4 and labetalol. Her BP suddenly spikes to 178/116, she reports a severe headache, and has 3+ reflexes with sustained clonus. The PRIORITY nursing action is:
options: Document findings and continue current medications
Notify the provider immediately—these findings suggest imminent eclampsia
rationale: The combination of acutely worsening hypertension, severe headache, and hyperreflexia with clonus are prodromal signs of imminent eclampsia. The nurse must notify the provider immediately so antihypertensive therapy can be escalated and delivery may be expedited. This is a medical emergency.

q: During a preeclampsia assessment, the nurse notes that the patient's peripheral smear shows burr cells and the LDH is 720. What should the nurse suspect?
options: Progression to HELLP syndrome with hemolysis
rationale: Burr cells on smear combined with LDH ≥600 are evidence of hemolysis—the "H" in HELLP. The nurse should urgently check platelets and AST/ALT to complete the HELLP evaluation. Schistocytes and burr cells are fragmented RBC forms indicating microangiopathic hemolytic anemia.

q: Which nursing intervention helps prevent aspiration during an eclamptic seizure?
options: Positioning the patient on her left side
Keeping the patient NPO for the entire admission
rationale: Left lateral positioning during and after a seizure allows secretions to drain from the mouth by gravity, preventing aspiration. Inserting anything into the mouth during a seizure is contraindicated. Left lateral also maximizes placental perfusion and prevents vena cava compression.

q: A patient has chronic hypertension and becomes pregnant. At 24 weeks, she develops proteinuria of 400 mg/24h and her BP increases from baseline 138/88 to 162/104. This represents:
options: Expected worsening of chronic hypertension in pregnancy
rationale: When a patient with pre-existing chronic hypertension develops new-onset proteinuria or worsening hypertension with end-organ signs after 20 weeks, this is superimposed preeclampsia. It carries higher risks than either condition alone and requires close monitoring and earlier delivery consideration.

q: A preeclamptic patient on bed rest asks why she needs to remain in the hospital rather than resting at home. The best nursing response is:
options: "Hospital policy requires all preeclamptic patients to be admitted."
"You need to stay because the doctor ordered it."
"Home rest would be fine, but insurance requires hospitalization."
"Your condition can rapidly worsen and may require immediate interventions including emergency delivery. Close monitoring protects you and your baby."
rationale: The therapeutic response explains the clinical rationale: preeclampsia can rapidly progress to severe disease, eclampsia, or HELLP. In-hospital monitoring allows for immediate detection of worsening symptoms, rapid initiation of MgSO4, antihypertensives, and emergent delivery if needed. This empowers the patient with knowledge.

q: Which nursing assessment finding on a patient with severe preeclampsia is MOST indicative of pulmonary edema?
options: Crackles on lung auscultation with dyspnea and decreasing SpO2
rationale: Crackles on auscultation, dyspnea, and decreasing oxygen saturation are classic signs of pulmonary edema, a severe feature of preeclampsia. The increased capillary permeability from endothelial dysfunction causes fluid to leak into alveoli. This requires immediate oxygen, possible diuresis, and emergent delivery.

q: A nurse is triaging multiple obstetric patients. Which patient requires the MOST immediate assessment?
options: A 34-week patient with HELLP syndrome, RUQ pain, and platelet count of 42,000
A 32-week patient with gestational hypertension and BP 144/92
A 36-week patient with mild preeclampsia requesting pain medication for a headache
A 38-week patient with chronic hypertension and stable vital signs
rationale: The HELLP patient with platelets of 42,000 and RUQ pain requires most immediate assessment. She is at high risk for hepatic rupture, DIC, and hemorrhage. This is an obstetric emergency requiring emergent delivery. The critically low platelet count means she may need transfusion before any surgical intervention.

q: A patient at 26 weeks with severe preeclampsia asks about the purpose of betamethasone injections. The nurse explains that this medication:
options: Lowers maternal blood pressure to safe levels
Accelerates fetal lung maturity in case early delivery is needed
Treats the underlying cause of preeclampsia
rationale: Betamethasone is a corticosteroid that accelerates fetal lung surfactant production to prepare the fetus for possible preterm delivery. It is given between 24-34 weeks when delivery may be necessary. It does not treat preeclampsia itself—delivery is the only cure.

q: A preeclamptic patient on MgSO4 complains of feeling flushed and warm. The nurse should:
options: Stop the infusion immediately as this indicates toxicity
Recognize flushing as a common side effect of MgSO4 (vasodilation) and continue monitoring
Administer calcium gluconate for an allergic reaction
Increase the infusion rate to reach therapeutic levels faster
rationale: Flushing and warmth are common expected effects of MgSO4 due to its vasodilatory action on smooth muscle. This is NOT a sign of toxicity. Signs of toxicity are absent DTRs, RR <12, and UO <30 mL/hr. Continue monitoring per the BURP protocol.

q: A nurse is reviewing the chart of a preeclamptic patient and notes an elevated indirect (unconjugated) bilirubin. This finding suggests:
options: Hemolysis of red blood cells consistent with HELLP progression
rationale: Elevated indirect (unconjugated) bilirubin indicates hemolysis—RBCs are being destroyed, releasing hemoglobin that is converted to unconjugated bilirubin. This is a marker of the 'H' in HELLP (Hemolysis). Direct (conjugated) bilirubin elevation would suggest obstructive causes.

q: The nurse understands that IVF (in vitro fertilization) pregnancy is classified as which type of risk factor for preeclampsia?
options: High-risk factor requiring aspirin with a single factor present
Moderate-risk factor requiring two or more factors for aspirin prophylaxis
rationale: IVF pregnancy is classified as a moderate-risk factor for preeclampsia. Moderate-risk factors require two or more to be present before aspirin prophylaxis is recommended. Other moderate-risk factors include nulliparity, obesity, age ≥35, family history, and African American race.

q: When assessing a patient with HELLP syndrome, the nurse checks for DIC. Which lab combination confirms DIC?
options: Decreased fibrinogen, prolonged PT/PTT, elevated D-dimer
Elevated fibrinogen, normal PT/PTT, normal D-dimer
Normal fibrinogen, normal PT/PTT, elevated hemoglobin
Elevated platelets, decreased LDH, normal bilirubin
rationale: DIC is characterized by decreased fibrinogen (consumed in widespread clotting), prolonged PT/PTT (clotting factors depleted), and elevated D-dimer (fibrin degradation products from clot breakdown). This triad indicates consumptive coagulopathy—a life-threatening complication of HELLP.

q: A preeclamptic patient at 37 weeks with no severe features is being prepared for delivery. The nurse understands the preferred method of delivery is:
options: Induction of labor with vaginal delivery preferred if no obstetric contraindications
Scheduled cesarean section for all preeclamptic patients
Emergency cesarean section regardless of cervical status
Expectant management until spontaneous labor occurs
rationale: For preeclampsia without severe features at 37 weeks, induction of labor with vaginal delivery is preferred if there are no obstetric contraindications. Cesarean is reserved for standard obstetric indications (failed induction, malpresentation, fetal distress). The goal is delivery of the placenta by the safest route.

q: A patient with severe preeclampsia suddenly develops right upper quadrant pain, shoulder pain, and hypotension. The nurse suspects:
options: Hepatic rupture—a catastrophic complication of HELLP/severe preeclampsia
rationale: RUQ pain with referred shoulder pain (diaphragmatic irritation from intraperitoneal blood) and hypotension (hemorrhagic shock) strongly suggest hepatic rupture—the most feared complication of HELLP. This requires emergency surgery, massive transfusion protocol, and surgical/interventional radiology consultation.
